summary: I was born in Wagner, South Dakota, and lived there until I was 17 years old, leaving in 1942. Wagner was my hometown during the Great Depression, and it shaped much of my early life. It’s a small place in South Dakota where my family roots run deep.

During the Great Depression, I worked at a defense plant to make some money before joining the Marines. Those were tough times for many families, including mine in South Dakota. The economic hardship influenced my decision to find work and later serve in the military.

My great-grandfather was Chief Running Bull of the Yankton Sioux tribe. He died in 1900, long before I was born, but his legacy was important to my family. Knowing about him connected me to my Native American heritage in South Dakota.

I am descended from the Yankton Sioux tribe, which is located in the southeast corner of South Dakota. My great-grandfather, Chief Running Bull, was a leader of this tribe. This heritage has always been a part of my identity growing up in South Dakota.

South Dakota is where I was born and raised until I left in 1942. It’s also where the Yankton Sioux tribe is located, connecting me to my Native American roots. My early years in South Dakota were during the Great Depression, shaping my outlook before I joined the Marines.

I joined the United States Marine Corps at age 18 in 1942 after working during the Depression. I trained on the West Coast at Camp Elliott in San Diego and served overseas in World War II. My time in the Marines was defined by combat in the Pacific and being part of elite units like the Fourth Marine Raider Battalion.

Camp Elliott in San Diego was where I went for boot camp and training after joining the Marines. I spent about four to five months there learning infantry and machine gun skills before shipping out overseas. It was my first step into military life on the West Coast.

San Diego was where I trained at Camp Elliott and later shipped out to the Pacific. After leaving Omaha, Nebraska, I took a train to San Diego before boarding the USS Polk. It was my last stop in the continental U.S. before heading into combat.

I first joined the Fourth Marine Raider Battalion when I went overseas in March 1944, although they had officially disbanded in February. Despite that, I was still assigned to them initially before being transferred to Chesty Puller’s A Company. This unit was part of my early combat experience in the Pacific.

Chesty Puller was the commander of A Company, which I was transferred to after the Fourth Marine Raider Battalion disbanded. I served under him throughout my time in the Marines, fighting in intense battles in the Pacific. His leadership was a significant part of my military experience.

I was assigned to A Company under Chesty Puller after the Fourth Marine Raider Battalion disbanded. I joined them in Omaha, Nebraska, and stayed with A Company throughout my Marine Corps service. We saw heavy combat in the Pacific, including a tough landing on an island with heavy enemy fire.

I joined A Company in Omaha, Nebraska before heading to the West Coast for deployment. Omaha was the place where I officially became part of that unit. From there, I took a train to San Diego and then shipped out to the Pacific theater.

My combat service took place across the Pacific Ocean, starting with deployment from San Diego to New Caledonia and then to the Russell Islands and Pavuvu. The Pacific was a vast and dangerous theater where I faced heavy fighting and amphibious landings. It was the main area of operations during my Marine Corps service in World War II.

New Caledonia was my first stop overseas after leaving San Diego on the USS Polk. We arrived there before moving on to the Russell Islands and Pavuvu for combat operations. It was part of the Pacific island chain where we staged before battle.

After New Caledonia, I was stationed at the Russell Islands as part of our Pacific campaign. This location was one of the stepping stones before we landed on Pavuvu and other combat zones. The islands were strategic points in the fight against the Japanese.

Pavuvu was one of the islands where I fought during the Pacific campaign. We landed there under heavy fire, facing a coral wall and intense Japanese resistance. The battle lasted about fifty days before the island was secured, and I spent nights alone in the swamp ahead of my unit.

I traveled overseas aboard the USS Polk, which was a troop carrier. We slept under a Higgins boat on the ship, sharing blankets with others including Bill Veeck. The USS Polk took us from San Diego to New Caledonia as part of our deployment to the Pacific theater.

Bill Veeck was on the USS Polk with me during our voyage to the Pacific. We slept under a Higgins boat and shared the same blanket. I remember he lost a leg, which was a notable part of his story during the war.

During the landing on the island, I saw a fellow Marine named Case who was a Mohawk Indian. He was in front of me and was killed early in the battle. His presence reminded me of the diverse backgrounds of the men fighting alongside me.

The Japanese were the enemy forces we fought against during the Pacific campaign. They dug caves and holes to wait for our artillery and fought fiercely during our landing on the island. I encountered them several times, including a tank near me in the swamp, and faced heavy opposition throughout the battle.
